algorithms.title: Pediatric Bradycardia Field Approach
algorithms.description: Sequence for recognizing and initially managing symptomatic pediatric bradycardia in the field.
steps: Confirm bradycardia for age and assess perfusion (color, capillary refill, mental status).
Assess airway and breathing first — most pediatric bradycardia is caused by hypoxia.
Support oxygenation and ventilation immediately (supplemental oxygen, assisted ventilation if indicated).
Consider other reversible causes if oxygenation/ventilation does not resolve bradycardia: increased vagal tone, hypothermia, increased intracranial pressure.

If bradycardia with poor perfusion persists despite adequate oxygenation and ventilation, this is a time-critical emergency — refer to the PALS reviewer for arrest-level bradycardia algorithms and drug dosing.

algorithms.title: Distinguishing and Responding to Pediatric Tachycardia
algorithms.description: Field approach to differentiating sinus tachycardia from SVT and responding appropriately.
steps: Identify the heart rate and assess for a clear triggering cause (fever, pain, anxiety, dehydration, shock).
If the rate is variable and a clear trigger is present: treat as sinus tachycardia by addressing the underlying cause (e.g., antipyretics for fever per protocol, fluids for dehydration/shock).
If the rate is fixed, very rapid (often >220 bpm in infants, >180 bpm in older children), and does not vary with stimulation or have a clear trigger: suspect SVT.
For suspected SVT with adequate perfusion: supportive care, monitor closely, rapid transport, notify receiving facility.

For suspected SVT with poor perfusion or altered mental status: this is a time-critical emergency — refer to the PALS reviewer for rhythm-specific interventions (e.g., vagal maneuvers, cardioversion, adenosine dosing).
